Those are the highest-value targets: long-lived nodes accumulate broad instance-role permissions and sit close to proprietary data. Any SSRF on such a node yields its credentials regardless of all the launch-time enforcement, and security teams may falsely believe 'IMDSv2 is enforced org-wide' because the policies are attached -- masking the unremediated existing fleet. EKS managed node groups and Auto Scaling fleets only get fixed when instances are replaced, so the exposure persists until a full roll.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/configuring-IMDS-new-instances.html -- 'Setting the account-level default does not reset existing instances ... existing instances that are set to IMDSv1 are not affected' and 'This setting does not change the IMDS version of existing instances, but blocks enabling IMDSv1 on existing instances that currently have IMDSv1 disabled'; remediation path is per-instance modify-instance-metadata-options at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/configuring-IMDS-existing-instances.html

probe: Lambda Function URL AuthType: AWS_IAM vs NONE (public-by-toggle footgun)
finding: Lambda Function URLs require an explicit AuthType of either AWS_IAM (SigV4-signed, IAM-authorized) or NONE (no Lambda-layer authentication; public if the resource policy grants Principal '*'). There is no implicit default - AuthType is mandatory at creation - but NONE is a single selectable value, and when you choose NONE via console or AWS SAM, Lambda AUTOMATICALLY creates the public 'FunctionURLAllowPublicAccess' resource policy (Principal '*') for you. Docs are blunt: 'any unauthenticated user with your function URL can invoke your function.' AWS provides real guardrails: an SCP example denying lambda:CreateFunctionUrlConfig/UpdateFunctionUrlConfig unless lambda:FunctionUrlAuthType=AWS_IAM, IAM Access Analyzer flags public/cross-account function URLs at no charge, and (from Oct 2025) new function URLs require BOTH lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl and lambda:InvokeFunction.
gap: A single AuthType=NONE selection, with the console auto-creating a Principal '*' policy, makes a Lambda directly internet-reachable with zero authentication - a classic one-toggle misconfiguration. Protection is opt-in: the SCP guardrail and IAM Access Analyzer must be deliberately enabled org-wide; neither is on by default. A team shipping an inference or internal-tool Lambda can expose it publicly without touching API Gateway, WAF, or a VPC.
risk: Proprietary model-serving or internal-IP Lambdas can be made publicly invokable by one parameter, bypassing every edge control. Without an org SCP pinning AWS_IAM, any of the many accounts under the Organization can do this; the cost-abuse and data-exfil surface is a raw, unthrottled, unauthenticated HTTPS endpoint.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/urls-auth.html — 'NONE - Lambda doesn't perform any authentication before invoking your function... Choose this option to allow public, unauthenticated access' and 'When you create a function URL with auth type NONE using the console or AWS SAM, Lambda automatically creates the resource-based policy for you.'

probe: API Gateway / HTTP API authorizers (IAM SigV4, Cognito, Lambda authorizers, JWT) and public-by-default-without-authorizer
finding: API Gateway offers a strong, layered authorizer set: IAM/SigV4 (request-signed, control-plane-grade), Lambda authorizers (token and request types; payload v1.0 for REST, v2.0 for HTTP), Cognito user pool authorizers, and - HTTP-API only - native JWT authorizers that validate OIDC/OAuth2 JWTs without custom code. IAM and Lambda authorizers reached HTTP APIs in 2020. However, an authorizer is opt-in per route/method: if none is attached, 'the request is sent directly to the Lambda function, regardless of who sent it' - i.e., the route is effectively public and each backend must self-authorize.
gap: No default-deny: creating an API with no authorizer on a route yields an open endpoint. Native JWT validation is HTTP-API only; REST APIs need a Lambda authorizer (custom code = a place to get token validation wrong). There is no account/org setting that forces every route to carry an authorizer; enforcement relies on review or custom Config/SCP rules.
risk: In a multi-team org, an internal API published without an authorizer is open to anyone who learns the invoke URL. The asymmetry (HTTP APIs get free JWT validation, REST APIs do not) pushes teams toward hand-rolled Lambda authorizers, where a signature/issuer/audience check mistake silently lets forged tokens through.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/http-api-jwt-authorizer.html (JWT authorizers, HTTP APIs only) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-use-lambda-authorizer.html (Lambda authorizers)

probe: API Gateway 'API keys' are explicitly NOT an authorization mechanism (separate from IAM)
finding: AWS is unusually transparent here. The docs state plainly: 'Don't use API keys for authentication or authorization to control access to your APIs. If you have multiple APIs in a usage plan, a user with a valid API key for one API in that usage plan can access ALL APIs in that usage plan.' API keys are 20-128 char static strings sent in headers (the docs note they 'can be logged'), are entirely separate from IAM credentials/control plane, and exist only to identify clients for usage-plan throttling and quotas. AWS directs you to IAM roles, Lambda authorizers, or Cognito for actual access control.
gap: The gap is ergonomic/human, not a doc gap: 'API key' is a term universally associated with authentication in other ecosystems, and the x-api-key header sits in the same request position as a bearer token, so teams routinely treat it as auth despite the warning. A static, loggable, cross-API-shared string offers no per-request signing and no rotation tie to IAM.
risk: A team that 'protects' an inference endpoint with only an API key has effectively no auth: the key grants access to every API sharing the usage plan, leaks via logs/proxies, and cannot be scoped per principal. Proprietary endpoints fronted this way are one header-leak away from open access.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-api-usage-plans.html — 'Don't use API keys for authentication or authorization to control access to your APIs... a user with a valid API key for one API in that usage plan can access all APIs in that usage plan.'

probe: SigV4 request-signing vs static credentials at the edge
finding: When API Gateway IAM authorization or a Lambda Function URL with AWS_IAM is used, callers must SigV4-sign each request: the signature is derived from the caller's IAM credentials plus request contents and is time-bound, so it is not a replayable static bearer secret and it is the SAME identity system as the control plane (one IAM principal, full CloudTrail attribution, conditions/SCPs apply). This is a genuine strength over static-token schemes - there is no long-lived shared secret transiting the wire, and invocation permission (lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl / execute-api:Invoke) is grantable independently of management permissions.
gap: SigV4 raises the integration bar: non-AWS clients (browsers, partner systems, many SDKs) cannot easily sign requests, which is exactly why teams fall back to NONE/API keys. IAM auth on a Function URL gives coarse allow/deny per principal but no built-in scoping of which payloads/paths a principal may call beyond what the resource policy expresses.
risk: The strongest edge-auth option (SigV4/IAM) is the least convenient, so under delivery pressure teams downgrade to public NONE or API keys for browser/partner traffic - trading the control-plane-grade, non-replayable model for a static or absent one on endpoints that front proprietary IP.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/urls-auth.html (AWS_IAM uses IAM identity + resource policy; lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl vs lambda:CreateFunctionUrlConfig/UpdateFunctionUrlConfig separate invocation from management)

probe: ALB OIDC/Cognito authentication and the header-spoofing / direct-target bypass footgun
finding: ALB can terminate authentication itself via authenticate-oidc / authenticate-cognito listener actions (HTTPS listeners only), running the OAuth2 flow and passing user claims to the target in an x-amzn-oidc-data JWT plus a session cookie. This is a real edge-auth layer requiring no app code for the login flow. But AWS's own best-practices blog flags two footguns: (1) the target must independently verify the JWT signature against the regional public key AND confirm the signer field equals the expected ALB ARN - otherwise an attacker can simply fabricate x-amzn-oidc-* headers; and (2) targets must be network-isolated (security group accepting traffic only from the ALB, private subnets) or clients reach the backend directly and bypass ALB auth entirely.
gap: ALB auth is fail-open by construction at the target: the load balancer does not prevent direct-to-target traffic, and it does not force the backend to validate the injected JWT. Both mitigations (signature verification + SG lockdown) are manual, opt-in, and easy to omit; nothing in the ALB config enforces them.
risk: An internal app 'secured' by ALB OIDC is wide open if its security group allows non-ALB sources or if it trusts x-amzn-oidc headers without signature checks - an attacker forges the identity header or hits the target IP directly, defeating SSO entirely. In a multi-team org with shared VPCs, the SG-misconfiguration path is realistic.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/networking-and-content-delivery/security-best-practices-when-using-alb-authentication/ — 'we strongly recommend your target applications verify the signature of the payload and validate that the signer field contains the expected ALB ARN' and guidance to restrict target security groups to the ALB.

probe: Multi-layer edge: CloudFront Functions vs Lambda@Edge before the function (defense-in-depth, with a key constraint)
finding: AWS supports stacking edge layers ahead of the compute: CloudFront (with WAF) -> optional CloudFront Functions or Lambda@Edge on viewer-request -> origin (API Gateway / ALB / Function URL) -> Lambda authorizer -> function. This is a genuine strength versus a bare exposed function: auth, JWT checks, and rewrites can run at PoPs before traffic reaches the backend. CloudFront Functions are sub-millisecond/JS-restricted and CANNOT reach KMS or Secrets Manager (any signing secret would have to live in source), so cryptographic JWT validation belongs in Lambda@Edge (up to 5s viewer-request) which has full runtime and secret access.
gap: The layers are all opt-in and independently configured; none is wired up by default. The CloudFront Functions vs Lambda@Edge split is a footgun: choosing the cheap/fast CF Functions for JWT validation invites embedding secrets in code or doing only weak token inspection. Lambda@Edge adds replication, regional cold starts, and version-management complexity that pushes teams to skip edge auth.
risk: Teams either under-build (single bare Function URL, no edge layer) or mis-build edge auth (secret-in-code CloudFront Function). The capability exists to put strong defense-in-depth in front of model/IP endpoints, but realizing it correctly is non-trivial and easy to get subtly wrong.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudFront/latest/DeveloperGuide/edge-functions-choosing.html (capability/limit differences) and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/networking-and-content-delivery/authorizationedge-how-to-use-lambdaedge-and-json-web-tokens-to-enhance-web-application-security/ (Authorization@Edge JWT pattern)

probe: AWS WAF is opt-in and must be explicitly associated (not default-on); fail-open on CloudFront vs fail-closed on Regional
finding: AWS WAF provides managed rule groups and custom rules, but a Web ACL protects nothing until it is EXPLICITLY associated with each resource (CloudFront distribution, API Gateway REST API, ALB, AppSync, Cognito user pool, App Runner, Amplify, Verified Access). There is no account-wide default WAF. A subtle, documented behavior: on a WAF internal error, 'CloudFront typically allows the request, while Regional services typically deny the request' - i.e., CloudFront-fronted resources fail OPEN, regional resources fail CLOSED. Firewall Manager can centrally enforce WAF across an Organization, but it is a separately enabled, paid add-on.
gap: No default protection: every new endpoint is WAF-less until someone associates an ACL, and there is no organization default that auto-attaches one (without buying Firewall Manager). The fail-open-on-CloudFront behavior means the most common public edge (CloudFront) can let requests through precisely during WAF malfunction - the opposite of what a security-sensitive workload wants.
risk: Across many accounts, newly launched APIs/distributions ship unprotected by default; coverage gaps are the norm unless Firewall Manager is funded and configured. The CloudFront fail-open nuance is easy to miss and undermines the assumption that 'WAF is in front, so we're protected.'
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/web-acl-associating-aws-resource.html (explicit per-resource association required) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/classic-web-acl-associating-cloudfront-distribution.html (CloudFront allows, Regional denies on WAF internal error)

probe: WAF rate-based rules for inference/GPU cost-abuse: real properties and the multi-minute detection lag
finding: WAF rate-based rules are the recommended abuse/cost-control mechanism. Since August 2024 the minimum limit is 10 requests (was 100), and since Mar 2024 the evaluation window is selectable (60/120/300/600s; default 300s). They can aggregate by IP, forwarded IP, custom keys, etc., and the action can be Block/Count/CAPTCHA/Challenge. BUT the docs explicitly state the mechanism 'is not intended for precise request-rate limiting': WAF estimates rate with a recency-weighted algorithm, 'it's possible for requests to be coming in at too high a rate for up to several minutes before AWS WAF detects and rate limits them,' and changing rate settings resets counters and can pause limiting for up to a minute.
gap: There is no hard, immediate request-rate ceiling at the WAF edge. The multi-minute detection lag is a sizable, documented hole for high-cost-per-request workloads: an attacker (or runaway client) can drive a large burst of GPU-backed inference calls before any block engages. WAF is also opt-in and must be associated first.
risk: For SageMaker/Bedrock/GPU-EC2 inference fronted by an API, several minutes of unthrottled flood per attack window can run up substantial compute spend before WAF reacts - the cost-abuse blast radius is bounded by detection lag, not by the configured limit. Rate limiting alone is insufficient; budget alarms and concurrency caps are needed alongside it.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-rate-based-caveats.html — 'it's possible for requests to be coming in at too high a rate for up to several minutes before AWS WAF detects and rate limits them.'

probe: API Gateway usage-plan throttling/quotas as cost control: AWS explicitly says don't rely on it
finding: Usage plans let you set per-API-key throttling (rate/burst) and quotas (requests per day/week/month). The capability reads like a cost/abuse guardrail, but AWS explicitly disclaims it: 'Usage plan throttling and quotas are not hard limits, and are applied on a best-effort basis. In some cases, clients can exceed the quotas that you set. Don't rely on usage plan quotas or throttling to control costs or block access to an API.' AWS redirects you to AWS Budgets (monitoring, not enforcement) and AWS WAF.
gap: The feature's surface vocabulary ('quota', 'throttle', 'limit') implies an enforced ceiling; the reality is best-effort and exceedable, and the recommended fallback (AWS Budgets) only alerts after spend, it does not stop requests. So there is no first-party edge control that hard-caps request volume or cost.
risk: A team that believes a usage-plan quota caps GPU/inference spend is mistaken - clients can blow past it, and Budgets only notifies post-hoc. Combined with WAF's multi-minute lag, there is no default mechanism that hard-stops cost-abuse against expensive endpoints; this requires app-level concurrency limits or provisioned-throughput caps.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-api-usage-plans.html — 'Usage plan throttling and quotas are not hard limits... Don't rely on usage plan quotas or throttling to control costs or block access to an API.'

probe: AWS Shield Standard (default-on, free) vs Shield Advanced (opt-in, paid)
finding: Shield Standard is genuinely default-on and free for all AWS customers, automatically defending against common L3/L4 (network/transport) DDoS at the edge (CloudFront, Route 53, Global Accelerator, ELB) with no enablement step. This is a real, unconditional strength. Shield Advanced is an opt-in paid tier ($3,000/month, 1-year commitment, plus data-transfer fees) that adds L7 detection/mitigation, near-real-time attack visibility, the DDoS Response Team, cost-protection credits, and tighter WAF integration.
gap: Standard covers L3/L4 only; sophisticated L7 (HTTP-flood/application-layer) attacks - the kind most relevant to expensive inference endpoints - require Shield Advanced or WAF rate rules, neither of which is default-on. The $3k/month + 1-year commitment makes Advanced a deliberate budget decision rather than a baseline.
risk: Volumetric L3/L4 protection is free and automatic (good baseline). But application-layer floods against GPU-backed APIs are out of scope for the free tier, so L7 abuse defense remains an explicit, paid, opt-in investment - leaving a default gap exactly where per-request cost is highest.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/ddos-standard-summary.html (Shield Standard, automatic/no cost) and https://aws.amazon.com/shield/pricing/ ($3,000/month Shield Advanced subscription)

probe: Edge observability: API Gateway access/execution logging and WAF logging are off by default
finding: The capability exists and is detailed: API Gateway supports independent execution logging and access logging to CloudWatch (and access logs to Kinesis Firehose), CloudFront supports standard/real-time logs, and WAF supports full request logging (to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Firehose) including requests blocked before they reach the function. But none are on by default. API Gateway logging additionally requires a one-time account-level IAM role (AmazonAPIGatewayPushToCloudWatchLogs) before any stage can log, then per-stage enablement; WAF logging must be turned on per Web ACL.
gap: Edge visibility is entirely opt-in and multi-step. A freshly deployed API has no access logs, no execution logs, and (absent WAF + WAF logging) no record of requests rejected at the edge. The account-level IAM-role prerequisite is a common reason logging silently does nothing even after a team thinks they enabled it.
risk: Without deliberately enabling these, an org cannot see who hit an endpoint fronting proprietary IP, cannot reconstruct an abuse/exfil incident at the edge, and loses the audit trail of edge-rejected requests. Across many accounts, default-off logging means investigation-blind endpoints unless centrally enforced.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/set-up-logging.html (execution + access logging are configured per stage and require an account-level CloudWatch role; not enabled by default)

probe: API Gateway request/response validation and payload limits at the edge (opt-in per method)
finding: API Gateway can validate required parameters/headers/query string and JSON body against models before invoking the backend, via request validators set with x-amazon-apigateway-request-validator at the API or per-method level. There is a hard, non-raisable 10 MB REST-API payload limit that does bound input size by default. But body validation is opt-in: simply defining a model does NOT enforce it - you must attach a validator to the method, and 'if no matching content type is found, request validation is not performed' (silent skip). Mapping templates can further transform/limit requests.
gap: Validation is default-off and silently skipped on content-type mismatch, so malformed/oversized-within-10MB or unexpected payloads reach the backend unless a validator is explicitly wired. The 10 MB cap helps, but it is the only validation that is on by default; structural validation requires deliberate model+validator configuration per method.
risk: Unvalidated payloads flow straight to inference/processing Lambdas, enlarging the attack and cost surface (oversized-but-under-10MB inputs, injection into downstream model calls). The silent no-validation-on-content-type-mismatch behavior can give a false sense that inputs are being checked when they are not.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-method-request-validation.html (validators are attached per method/API; defining a model alone does not validate) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/limits.html (10 MB payload limit)

probe: Cognito identity-pool unauthenticated (guest) roles: default state, the 'scope-down' session policy that supposedly protects anonymous callers, and whether that protection is bypassed by the basic/classic flow
finding: Guest access is OFF by default and AWS explicitly recommends leaving it deactivated: 'Anyone who knows your identity pool ID can request unauthenticated credentials. Your identity pool ID isn't confidential... When you activate guest access, the AWS permissions that you grant to unauthenticated sessions are available to everyone.' When guest access IS enabled, AWS markets a protective 'scope-down policy' for unauthenticated identities. Two important footguns hide here. (1) The protection only applies to the ENHANCED flow (GetCredentialsForIdentity). In the BASIC/classic flow the app composes its own AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity call and 'these restrictions' are not auto-applied; AWS only documents them as a 'best security practice.' (2) The scope-down ceiling itself is broad, not tight: the AWS-managed session policy AmazonCognitoUnAuthedIdentitiesSessionPolicy plus the inline session policy permit anonymous guests up to s3:*, dynamodb:*, lambda:*, sqs:*, execute-api:*, kms:Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey*, and notably sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint on Resource '*' (the effective grant is the INTERSECTION with the role's own policy, so the ceiling only bites if the role policy is over-broad). IAM does enforce one real guardrail: identity-pool roles cannot be saved without a cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:aud trust condition, and AWS STS fails cross-account basic-flow AssumeRole if the aud condition is absent. But the amr=='unauthenticated' restriction that actually separates guests from authenticated users is NOT required by IAM and must be added by the operator.
gap: The 'scope-down policy' is a familiar-sounding safety term that implies a tight cage but is actually a wide allowlist of ~30 service families (including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, KMS, and SageMaker InvokeEndpoint) that only constrains via intersection with the role policy. A team that attaches an over-permissioned role to the unauthenticated identity (e.g., s3:GetObject on a proprietary bucket, or lambda:InvokeFunction on an internal backend) hands those exact permissions to any anonymous internet caller who knows the non-secret pool ID. The protection is also entirely absent in the basic flow, which teams are explicitly told to switch to whenever they need a service outside the allowlist. The required-by-IAM trust condition only pins the pool (aud), not the authentication state (amr) - so the one guardrail IAM enforces does not stop a guest from assuming an authenticated-intended role if amr isn't also conditioned.
risk: For a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, a single misconfigured identity pool is a direct, unauthenticated public-internet path to AWS credentials: read source/model-weight S3 objects, invoke an internal Lambda, query a DynamoDB table, or call a SageMaker inference endpoint - all without any login. Because pool IDs are non-secret and embedded in client apps, discovery is trivial; the only thing standing between the internet and the backend is whether each team wrote a correctly least-privilege guest role and remembered the amr condition.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/iam-roles.html (sections 'Services that unauthenticated users can access', 'The AWS managed session policy for guests' listing AmazonCognitoUnAuthedIdentitiesSessionPolicy with sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint/kms:*, and 'Note: In the basic (classic) flow, you make your own AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity API request, and can apply these restrictions to the request'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/identity-pools-security-best-practices.html ('leave guest access deactivated'; 'available to everyone')

probe: Cognito user-pool threat protection (formerly Advanced Security Features): compromised-credential detection and adaptive risk-based MFA - default-on vs opt-in, paid-tier gating, and enforcement mode
finding: Threat protection (compromised-credentials detection, adaptive risk-based MFA/blocking, IP allow/deny lists, and per-session risk logging) exists and is genuinely capable. But it is triple-gated rather than default-on. (1) It is only available in the paid 'Plus' feature plan; AWS states plainly 'Threat protection is available in the Plus feature plan.' A user pool on Lite or Essentials has no access to it. (2) Even after activation it starts in 'audit-only' mode - it monitors and emits CloudWatch metrics but applies no mitigations; AWS recommends running audit-only for 'two weeks or more' before enabling 'full-function' mode (AdvancedSecurityMode=ENFORCED). (3) Coverage has real blind spots independent of configuration: compromised-credentials checking requires plaintext-password flows (USER_PASSWORD_AUTH/ADMIN_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH) and explicitly does NOT work for USER_SRP_AUTH, custom auth, federated sign-in, or machine-to-machine (client-credentials) clients. Adaptive auth also does not apply rate limits, so volumetric/credential-stuffing-by-volume defense requires separately attaching an AWS WAF web ACL.
gap: The capability is strong but off by default along three axes (paid tier not selected, audit-only on activation, and unsupported for SRP/federated/M2M). The 2025 repackaging of 'advanced security features' into the 'Plus' tier is a familiar-vocabulary trap: an org may assume a standard user pool resists credential stuffing and account takeover when, unless someone explicitly upgraded the tier AND moved past audit-only AND uses a password flow that supports it, no mitigation is actually being enforced. The SRP exclusion is especially easy to miss because SRP is the recommended/default secure sign-in flow - using the more secure transport silently disables compromised-credential detection.
risk: For a multi-team org, user pools fronting access to proprietary backends can sit in a state where they detect nothing and block nothing - either because the team never selected the paid Plus plan, never advanced past audit-only, or uses SRP/federated/M2M auth that threat protection cannot inspect. Account takeover via leaked/reused credentials then proceeds undetected, and volumetric credential stuffing requires a separately-provisioned WAF that is not part of the user pool by default.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/cognito-user-pool-settings-threat-protection.html ('Threat protection is available in the Plus feature plan'; 'Threat protection starts out in an audit-only mode... As a best practice, run in audit-only mode for two weeks or more before you enable full-function mode'; 'You can't use threat protection with federated sign-in'; 'Threat protection doesn't affect M2M requests'; 'Threat protection doesn't apply rate limits'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/feature-plans-features-plus.html (threat protection lives only in the Plus plan)

probe: AWS CodeCommit (first-party Git source control) availability for new customers
finding: AWS CodeCommit RETURNED to full general availability on November 24, 2025: new customer sign-ups are open again via the Console, CLI, and APIs, and AWS reversed its 2024 no-new-features stance — it is investing in the service again, with Git LFS support announced for Q1 2026 and regional expansion (eu-south-2, ca-west-1) starting Q3 2026. The earlier closure was real (onboarding ceased ~July 25, 2024; existing customers retained) but is no longer the current state. A multi-team org standing up fresh accounts under its Organization CAN once again use a first-party, IAM-native Git host: CodeCommit repositories can be created directly in any account, with access governed entirely by IAM and encryption via KMS — no external credential and no widening of the trust boundary beyond AWS IAM.
gap: CodeCommit is once again a viable IAM-native, in-account Git host, removing the prior forced dependency on an external SCM for new accounts. Residual considerations: (1) the 2024–2025 closure means many orgs already standardized on GitHub/GitLab via CodeConnections and may not migrate back; (2) CodeCommit's collaboration feature set still trails GitHub/GitLab (Git LFS only arriving Q1 2026, no rich PR-automation/marketplace ecosystem), so it suits IAM-centric internal repositories better than a full developer-collaboration platform.
risk: Low/structural: keeping proprietary source in an IAM-native host keeps the supply-chain trust boundary inside AWS IAM. The main risk is organizational — betting on a service that AWS shuttered to new customers for ~16 months — and feature-parity gaps that may push teams to external SCM anyway, re-expanding the trust boundary.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/devops/aws-codecommit-returns-to-general-availability/ (returned to GA Nov 24, 2025); prior closure: CodeCommit User Guide closure banner (~July 25, 2024)

probe: CodeConnections (formerly CodeStar Connections) GitHub App vs PAT, and repo-level scoping
finding: CodePipeline/CodeBuild integrate with GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket via AWS CodeConnections using the 'AWS Connector for GitHub' GitHub App rather than a broad personal access token (PAT). This is the strong pattern: GitHub App installations are repo-selectable. The CodePipeline console explicitly states 'you can select specific repositories under Repository Access. Any repositories that are not selected will not be accessible or visible by CodePipeline.' Security Hub control CodeBuild.1 actively flags source repository URLs that embed a PAT or username/password as Critical, steering users off PATs and onto the connection model.
gap: Repo selection is enforced on the GitHub side (the App installation), not via an AWS-side IAM construct, so the scoping decision lives in the GitHub org and is invisible to AWS IAM policy. The connection itself is created only by the GitHub org owner / repo owner, concentrating the authorization decision in one GitHub admin.
risk: For a multi-team org, repo scoping is correct and credit-worthy, but the control point is the GitHub App installation config, not an AWS guardrail — a misconfigured 'All repositories' install silently grants every AWS account sharing that App access to all source, including proprietary model weights repos.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/latest/userguide/connections-github.html (Repository Access note); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/codebuild-controls.html (CodeBuild.1)

probe: CodeConnections GitHub App permission breadth and the single-install-per-org constraint (read/write to supply chain)
finding: Although the App is repo-selectable, on the repos it covers the AWS Connector for GitHub requests broad read AND write permissions: read/write to code, commit statuses, pull requests, repository administration, and repository/organization webhooks — not read-only. GitHub permits only ONE installation of a given App per GitHub organization, so every CodeConnection from every AWS account in the Organization that targets that org shares one identical App installation and its permission set; there is no AWS-side facility to scope a specific connection down to a subset of the App's granted repos or to read-only. A connection can be shared across AWS accounts, widening blast radius further.
gap: The integration is not least-privilege: it has write access to the source supply chain (code, branch protection via admin, CI workflows, webhooks) when most pipelines only need read/clone. There is no read-only connection mode and no AWS IAM-level scope-down of which repos a given connection token can reach — 'you don't get any further controls to scope down its access within that particular AWS account.' One shared App across accounts means compromise of any one AWS account that can call the connection can read or tamper with all covered repos.
risk: Compromise of a single AWS account/principal able to use a connection (e.g. via codeconnections:UseConnection / GetConnectionToken) yields write access to proprietary source: inject code, remove branch protection, register rogue self-hosted runners. For a multi-team IP-holding org this is a supply-chain-wide blast radius from one account compromise.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codebuild/latest/userguide/connections-github-app.html (App requires webhook + repo permission scopes); analysis of App scopes at https://thomaspreece.com/2025/12/04/part-1-overview-of-aws-codeconnections-escalating-privileges-via-aws-codeconnections/ ; cross-account sharing at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dtconsole/latest/userguide/connections-share.html

probe: Auditing source/supply-chain access via the connection (CloudTrail coverage of token vend vs git fetch)
finding: CodeConnections is integrated with CloudTrail and logs management-plane API calls — CreateConnection, GetConnection, DeleteConnection, CreateHost, UseConnection, etc. Every example in the CloudTrail logging doc is eventCategory 'Management' (managementEvent: true). CloudTrail records who created/used a connection, source IP, and time. However, the doc surfaces no data-event for the credential-vend (GetConnectionToken) nor any record of which repository content was actually cloned/fetched once a token is issued — those git operations happen against GitHub, not against an AWS API, so AWS CloudTrail cannot see the actual read/write of repo content. The org must rely on the GitHub side (audit log / git events) for that half.
gap: There is a split-brain audit story: AWS CloudTrail proves a token was vended/connection used, but the actual repository access (which files, which commits, push vs pull) is only visible in the GitHub audit log. There is no single pane correlating 'AWS principal X used connection Y to clone repo Z' end to end, and GetConnectionToken is not exposed as a configurable CloudTrail data event for fine-grained alerting.
risk: Detecting and forensically reconstructing exfiltration of proprietary source via a connection requires joining AWS CloudTrail with GitHub audit logs; gaps or retention mismatch between the two break the investigation. The most security-relevant call (token vend that unlocks repo access) is not a first-class data event.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dtconsole/latest/userguide/logging-using-cloudtrail-connections.html (all listed events are eventCategory: Management; no GetConnectionToken data-event example)

probe: Keyless BYO CI/CD: GitHub Actions OIDC -> sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity (recommended strong pattern)
finding: AWS documents and recommends the keyless pattern: register token.actions.githubusercontent.com as an IAM OIDC identity provider, create an IAM role whose trust policy allows sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, and use aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials to exchange a per-run GitHub OIDC JWT for short-lived STS credentials. The AWS Security blog explicitly frames this as removing the need for long-lived IAM user access keys, aligning with the Well-Architected Security Pillar's 'temporary and limited-privilege credentials.' This lets a team run its own CI on GitHub and push artifacts to AWS WITHOUT granting AWS any access to source — source never enters AWS.
gap: The strong pattern exists and is documented; the gap is ergonomic/footgun, addressed in the next probe (trust-policy condition scoping).
risk: Low when implemented correctly — this is the recommended path for a multi-team org that wants to keep proprietary source on GitHub and only hand AWS keyless, short-lived, role-scoped access. Credit-worthy.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-iam-roles-to-connect-github-actions-to-actions-in-aws/ ; GitHub side: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/security-hardening-your-deployments/configuring-openid-connect-in-amazon-web-services

probe: OIDC trust-policy scoping footgun: missing/wildcard sub-claim condition (confused deputy)
finding: The keyless OIDC pattern is only safe if the IAM role trust policy constrains the token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub (and :aud) claim to a specific repo/branch, e.g. 'repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main'. A trust policy that omits the sub condition lets ANY GitHub repository on the public Actions OIDC issuer assume the role (a documented 2023-era confused-deputy class of finding); a StringLike wildcard like 'repo:org/*' grants every repo in the org — including throwaway/PoC repos any developer can create. AWS's own Security blog shows only a correctly-scoped single example and does NOT warn against the wildcard/omitted-sub failure mode; the caution lives in third-party security writeups and GitHub's docs, not prominently in AWS's. GitHub later added immutable claims like repository_id to harden against rename-based bypass.
gap: AWS makes the correct configuration possible but not ergonomically fail-safe: the IAM trust policy will happily accept an OIDC provider with no sub condition (role assumable by any repo) or a broad org wildcard, with no AWS-side warning, linter, or default guardrail. The aud claim alone (sts.amazonaws.com) is not repo-binding. The safe pattern depends entirely on the author hand-writing a tight StringEquals/StringLike on sub.
risk: A single mis-scoped role in any team account becomes an org-wide entry point: an external or low-trust repo can assume a role that may reach proprietary S3 buckets (model weights, source artifacts) or ECR. The footgun is easy to hit and AWS's primary doc does not flag it.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-iam-roles-to-connect-github-actions-to-actions-in-aws/ (shows scoped sub example but no explicit wildcard warning); failure mode documented at https://www.wiz.io/blog/avoiding-mistakes-with-aws-oidc-integration-conditions and https://medium.com/tinder/identifying-vulnerabilities-in-github-actions-aws-oidc-configurations-8067c400d5b8

probe: CodeBuild build execution isolation: ephemeral environments, VPC option, privileged/Docker exposure
finding: CodeBuild on-demand fleets provision compute per build and DESTROY the machine when the build finishes (ephemeral, no reuse across builds by default), which limits cross-build contamination. Builds can be attached to a customer VPC for private access to RDS/ElastiCache/VPC endpoints. Docker-in-Docker requires explicitly opting into 'privileged mode'; privileged mode is OFF by default ('By default, Docker containers do not allow access to any devices'). When enabled, privileged mode is effectively root-on-the-build-host, but AWS isolates the underlying VM so there is no documented horizontal path to other tenants' resources. Reserved-capacity fleets (opt-in) deliberately persist hosts to cut latency, which trades isolation for speed.
gap: Default ephemerality and default-off privileged mode are good. The footgun is reserved-capacity fleets, where hosts persist and a malicious or leaky build can affect a later build on the same warm host — a trade-off the user opts into for performance, and the isolation guarantees of reserved fleets are less emphasized than the latency benefit.
risk: For most pipelines, default isolation is sound. Risk concentrates where teams enable privileged mode (needed to build Docker images) and/or reserved fleets to speed up GPU/large builds — a compromised build then runs as root on a host that may serve subsequent builds, a plausible scenario for an AI/IP shop building many container images.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codebuild/latest/userguide/fleets.html (on-demand fleets destroyed after build; VPC connectivity); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/codebuild-controls.html (CodeBuild.5: privilegedMode default false, privileged grants access to all devices)

probe: Build-secret exposure in CodeBuild (env vars, build logs, source URL credential leakage)
finding: CodeBuild supports pulling secrets from SSM Parameter Store / Secrets Manager via the buildspec rather than inlining them, and Security Hub ships Critical-severity controls that detect the two classic leaks: CodeBuild.2 fails if a project defines plaintext AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY env vars, and CodeBuild.1 fails if a (Bitbucket) source repo URL embeds a PAT or username/password. CodeBuild.4 requires at least one log destination (CloudWatch/S3) and CodeBuild.3 wants those S3 logs encrypted. The detective controls exist but are opt-in (Security Hub must be enabled and the standard turned on); plaintext env vars and secrets echoed into build logs are not blocked by default.
gap: The guardrails are detective and opt-in, not preventive and default-on. CodeBuild does not by default redact secrets from build logs, does not block plaintext credential env vars at create time, and the CodeBuild.5 privileged-mode control was RETIRED by Security Hub in April 2024 — so there is no longer a managed control flagging privileged-mode projects. A buildspec that echoes a secret to stdout or a Dockerfile that bakes a secret into an exfiltratable image layer is not caught without extra tooling.
risk: Proprietary build secrets (registry creds, signing keys, model-repo tokens) can leak into CloudWatch/S3 build logs or into ECR image layers and go undetected unless Security Hub is enabled and the org adds its own log-scrubbing and image scanning. The retirement of the privileged-mode control removes a previously-managed signal.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/codebuild-controls.html (CodeBuild.1 Critical PAT-in-URL; CodeBuild.2 Critical plaintext AWS creds; CodeBuild.3 S3 log encryption; CodeBuild.4 logging enabled)

probe: BYO prebuilt images to ECR without giving AWS source access (decoupled supply chain)
finding: Teams can fully decouple AWS from their source by building images in their own CI (e.g. GitHub Actions) and pushing prebuilt images to Amazon ECR using the keyless OIDC role pattern (ecr:GetAuthorizationToken + push perms scoped to a repo), so AWS never sees source. ECR supports image scanning and immutable tags. Combined with the OIDC pattern, this is a documented, IAM-scopable way to feed AWS deployment from external CI without a CodeConnection or any AWS-side repo grant.
gap: This is the lowest-source-exposure path and is well supported; its safety inherits the OIDC trust-policy scoping footgun (a too-broad sub claim on the ECR-push role lets the wrong repo push images). ECR enhanced scanning and tag immutability are themselves opt-in, not default.
risk: Low source-exposure but the integrity of the artifact path depends on a tight push-role trust policy and on enabling ECR immutability/scanning; a loose OIDC condition could let an untrusted repo push a poisoned image into the deployment supply chain for proprietary workloads.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-iam-roles-to-connect-github-actions-to-actions-in-aws/ (OIDC role -> AWS services incl. ECR push); ECR auth: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/registry_auth.html

probe: End-to-end build provenance and downstream signature verification enforcement across CodeBuild/CodePipeline (Signer signing, ECR/EKS verify, source-commit integrity)
finding: AWS has all the pieces but none is a default-on, CodeBuild-native provenance gate. Signing is do-it-yourself: the buildspec reference (version 0.2) has NO native 'provenance', 'attestation', 'in-toto' or SLSA primitive — you install the Notation CLI in pre_build and run `notation sign` against an AWS Signer profile yourself (per AWS Security blog 'Best Practices to help secure your container image build pipeline by using AWS Signer'). The SLSA-v1.1-compatible provenance that third parties attribute to CodeBuild is produced by tooling run INSIDE the build, not by a verified CodeBuild attestation the platform stamps and that downstream actions trust. Well-Architected DevOps Guidance [DL.CS.2] 'Sign code artifacts after each build' is explicitly category RECOMMENDED (advisory), not enforced. Downstream verification IS available and reasonably strong when configured: Amazon ECR managed signing + AWS Signer, and EKS native signature verification / Ratify-style admission controllers and ECR repo policies can BLOCK_ON_FAILURE — but every layer is opt-in (ECR managed signing and EKS verification are configured per-registry/per-cluster, default audit-or-off). Source-side integrity is the weakest link: the CodeStarSourceConnection (CodeConnections) source action surfaces a CommitId/CommitMessage output variable but performs NO commit-signature or provenance verification — it trusts whatever commit the connection reports on the configured branch, DetectChanges defaults to true, and OutputArtifactFormat defaults to CODE_ZIP (a shallow copy); full git metadata is only available via the opt-in CODEBUILD_CLONE_REF format used by a downstream CodeBuild action. So 'who/what produced this artifact' is not cryptographically bound from commit -> build -> deploy unless the team assembles Signer signing, ECR/EKS verification, and their own commit-signing discipline themselves.
gap: There is no single AWS-native control that asserts and enforces 'this artifact was produced by build X from commit Y reviewed via PR Z' and refuses to deploy otherwise. SLSA-style verified provenance is bring-your-own (run Notation/in-toto tooling in the buildspec); the platform does not stamp a trusted attestation that CodePipeline deploy actions automatically verify. Signing, ECR managed signing, EKS/Ratify admission, and commit-signature checks are independently opt-in, default off-or-audit, and must be stitched together by each team. The source action's lack of commit-signature verification means a pipeline will happily build and ship an unsigned/force-pushed commit.
risk: For a multi-team org guarding proprietary IP (source, model weights, internal docs), the chain from source to running container can ship a tampered artifact with no platform-enforced provenance: an attacker who can push to a watched branch (or who controls a build that mutates artifacts) produces a deploy that nothing automatically rejects, because verification is off-by-default at the source action, at ECR, and at the EKS/ECS admission layer unless every team turned each one on. The capability exists, but the secure outcome depends entirely on each team's assembly and is not the default.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/best-practices-to-help-secure-your-container-image-build-pipeline-by-using-aws-signer/ ; buildspec has no native provenance/attestation field: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codebuild/latest/userguide/build-spec-ref.html ; advisory-only signing guidance: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/devops-guidance/dl.cs.2-sign-code-artifacts-after-each-build.html ; source action does no commit verification, CODE_ZIP default: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/latest/userguide/action-reference-CodestarConnectionSource.html ; ECR/EKS verification enforcement (opt-in, BLOCK vs LOG): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/image-signing-verification.html and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/announcing-container-image-signing-with-aws-signer-and-amazon-eks/

probe: Blast radius of the CodeBuild execution role and the CodeConnections token: can a poisoned build assume deploy roles, read cross-team secrets, or push to source?
finding: The default CodeBuild service role policy AWS generates ships with `Resource: "*"` on logs:*, s3:GetObject/PutObject/GetObjectVersion, the ECR pull actions, and s3:GetBucketAcl/GetBucketLocation — AWS itself flags this with the note 'This policy contains statements that allow access to a potentially large number of AWS resources... change the value of the Resource array.' Crucially, sts:AssumeRole (to reach deploy roles), secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, and ssm:GetParameters are NOT in the default — they are added per-use-case by the operator, so a build can assume a broad deploy role or read another team's secrets only if someone wrote that grant, but nothing structurally confines a team's build role to that team's resources by default. The confused-deputy guidance (aws:SourceAccount / aws:SourceArn condition keys on the trust policy) is opt-in and shown only as a 'recommended' variant. The sharper, less-documented footgun is CodeConnections: the connection App holds read/WRITE/ADMIN on all repos in the org, and a CodeBuild job can call the (undocumented) GetBuildInfo path / codeconnections:GetConnectionToken to extract the raw GitHub `ghs_` / Bitbucket token, which inherits the App's admin scope — letting a poisoned build disable branch protection, remove required PR reviews, add repo admins, or push to OTHER teams' repos, operating directly against GitHub/Bitbucket APIs outside AWS CloudTrail. GetConnectionToken has no condition keys to scope it, so the only complete mitigation is an org SCP denying codeconnections:GetConnectionToken / codestar-connections:GetConnectionToken.
gap: No default tenancy boundary on a build role tied to a team/account, and no condition-key surface on codeconnections:GetConnectionToken to restrict which build/project can mint a source-provider token. The connection token bypasses AWS-side IAM/logging entirely (acts as the GitHub App), so AWS CloudTrail does not see what the token does on GitHub — and legitimate builds make many token calls, masking exfiltration. Least privilege CAN be written for the build role, but the CodeConnections admin-scoped token cannot be scoped down within AWS short of an org-level SCP block.
risk: A single compromised build in one team's account becomes a cross-team software-supply-chain pivot: extract the connection token -> disable branch protection / required reviews on another team's repo -> push poisoned IaC or workflow code -> that code is then built and deployed (with no platform provenance gate, per the first finding) into production handling proprietary IP and model weights. Because the token operates outside AWS, the lateral movement is invisible to CloudTrail-based detection. Containment depends on org guardrails (SCP) and hand-written role scoping, not on safe defaults.
evidence: Default service role is wildcard-scoped and warned-but-permissive: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codebuild/latest/userguide/setting-up-service-role.html ; CodePipeline service role least-privilege/scope-down guidance: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/latest/userguide/how-to-custom-role.html ; CodeConnections token extraction / admin scope / SCP-only mitigation: https://thomaspreece.com/2026/03/23/part-2-aws-codebuild-escalating-privileges-via-aws-codeconnections/ and connection App scope: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/latest/userguide/connections-github.html

probe: Account hierarchy and isolation unit (Organization -> OU -> Account); no in-account project/folder layer
finding: AWS Organizations provides a 3-level structural hierarchy: one Organization root -> OUs (max 2000 OUs, nestable up to 5 levels deep under a root) -> Accounts. The account is the hard isolation boundary: IAM, resource quotas, billing, and blast radius are all scoped to an account. There is no sub-account 'project' or 'folder' construct like GCP projects/folders. Default cap is 10 accounts per org, adjustable via Service Quotas up to 50,000. Created accounts must exist 4 days before removal; only 5 accounts can be created concurrently. OUs are policy-attachment targets, not isolation boundaries (an OU does not isolate IAM or network).
gap: For a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, isolation forces an account-per-team-per-environment sprawl: there is no lighter-weight intra-account partition, so 'many teams' becomes 'many hundreds of accounts' to get clean blast-radius/IAM separation. The 5-concurrent-account-creation and 4-day-minimum-age limits slow programmatic vending. OUs look like a folder layer but isolate nothing on their own.
risk: Account proliferation increases the surface area for misconfiguration drift (each account is a fresh place to forget GuardDuty, S3 Block Public Access, or logging). Teams under-provision accounts to avoid sprawl and end up co-locating sensitive IP (source, weights) with general workloads in one account, weakening the only real isolation boundary.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_reference_limits.html (Maximum number of accounts: 10, adjustable to 50,000; Number of OUs in an organization: 2000; 'OU maximum nesting in a root: Five levels of OUs deep under a root.')

probe: Service Control Policies (SCPs): default FullAWSAccess, exemptions, and the resource-based-policy/external-principal blind spot
finding: SCPs are deny/allow guardrails on the maximum permissions of IAM principals in MEMBER accounts. They are deny-list by default: every root/OU/account gets a default FullAWSAccess SCP; you must keep it and layer Deny statements (removing it blocks all actions). Documented hard exemptions: (1) SCPs do NOT affect users/roles in the MANAGEMENT account, (2) SCPs do NOT affect service-linked roles, (3) several root-user tasks (Enterprise Support signup, CloudFront trusted signer, Lightsail/EC2 reverse DNS). Critically, SCPs only constrain principals belonging to the org's accounts; they 'don't affect resource-based policies directly' and 'don't affect users or roles from accounts outside the organization' -- e.g., an S3 bucket policy granting access to an external account is not constrained by the bucket-owner's SCP.
gap: Two well-architected gaps: (1) the management account is exempt -- privileged operators and any roles there bypass org guardrails, so least-privilege cannot be written for the most powerful account via SCP. (2) SCPs historically could not stop a resource (S3 bucket, KMS key) from being shared OUT to an external/untrusted principal -- the classic data-exfiltration perimeter hole. This is precisely the gap RCPs were created to fill.
risk: For an org guarding proprietary source code and model weights in S3/KMS, an SCP alone cannot enforce a data perimeter against external sharing; a single overly-broad bucket or key policy in a member account can expose IP to an outside account regardless of SCPs. Management-account exemption means a compromised or careless management-account role is unbounded.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html ('SCPs don't affect users or roles in the management account'; 'SCPs do not affect any service-linked role'; 'SCPs don't affect resource-based policies directly. They also don't affect users or roles from accounts outside the organization.')

probe: Resource Control Policies (RCPs) -- launched Nov 2024 to close the external-principal/resource-policy gap
finding: RCPs (GA Nov 13, 2024) are a new org policy type that caps the maximum permissions ON RESOURCES regardless of which principal (including external/cross-org principals and root) calls them -- the complement to SCPs. RCPFullAWSAccess is auto-attached to root/every OU/every account and cannot be detached (counts toward the 5-RCP-per-entity quota); no restrictions exist by default, so RCPs are opt-in to author. They enforce even when a resource-based policy grants external access. BUT scope is limited: at the time of writing RCPs apply to a subset of ~24 services -- notably S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager, plus DynamoDB, ECR, CloudWatch Logs, Cognito, etc. RCPs do NOT affect the management account, do NOT affect service-linked roles, and do NOT apply to AWS-managed KMS keys.
gap: RCPs are the right primitive for a data perimeter, but (a) coverage is a curated allow-list of services, not universal -- EBS, EFS, RDS, SageMaker, Bedrock, ECS/EKS resources are NOT in scope, so IP stored or processed there gets no RCP perimeter; (b) the management account remains exempt; (c) like SCPs they are opt-in and require careful authoring (AWS explicitly warns not to attach to org root without testing). 'RCP' sounds like a universal resource guardrail but it is service-gated.
risk: An org can build a tight S3/KMS/Secrets-Manager perimeter against external sharing, but model weights on EBS/EFS, training data in SageMaker, or RDS databases fall outside RCP coverage -- the perimeter has named holes. Teams may assume 'RCP = all resources protected' and leave non-covered services exposed.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_rcps.html ('List of AWS services that support RCPs' enumerates S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager, DynamoDB, ECR, CloudWatch Logs, Cognito, etc.; 'RCPs don't affect resources in the management account'; 'RCPs do not impact the effective permissions of any service-linked role'; 'RCPs do not apply to AWS managed keys')

probe: Declarative Policies (launched Dec 2024) -- durable desired-state config enforcement vs SCP action-blocking
finding: Declarative policies (announced Dec 2024) are a new org policy type that enforces a desired baseline CONFIGURATION that persists even as AWS adds new APIs/features or new accounts join -- distinct from SCPs which block specific actions and require an exception for every new API. At launch they cover EC2, EBS, and VPC only. Enforceable attributes include: require IMDSv2, EBS encryption-by-default, block public access for EBS snapshots and AMIs, allowed-AMI settings, VPC Block Public Access, and serial-console access. They ship with an 'account status report' (summary + detailed CSV across accounts/regions) to assess readiness before enforcing. Attachable at org/OU/account; up to 10 per entity, 10,000-char limit, 1000 per org.
gap: Powerful for baseline hardening but narrow: only EC2/EBS/VPC at launch ('support for other services coming soon'). The headline capabilities most relevant to IP protection -- IMDSv2 enforcement, EBS encryption by default, VPC/AMI/snapshot public-access blocks -- are all OPT-IN to author, not default-on. There is no declarative policy for S3, IAM, KMS, SageMaker/Bedrock configuration baselines yet.
risk: Without authoring declarative policies, the common GPU/EC2 footguns (IMDSv1 SSRF-to-credential-theft, unencrypted EBS holding weights, public AMI/snapshot leaks) remain reachable in every account by default. The feature exists but delivers nothing until explicitly configured, and its scope excludes the storage/AI services where IP most often lives.
evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/simplify-governance-with-declarative-policies/ ('it is maintained, even as new features or new APIs are added'; supports EC2, VPC, EBS; account status report generates 'a summary view and a detailed CSV file') and https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2024/12/aws-declarative-policies/

probe: Centralized root access management for member accounts (launched Nov 2024)
finding: IAM now lets the org management account (or a delegated IAM admin) centrally remove long-term root credentials (password, access keys, signing certs, MFA) from MEMBER accounts and disallow their recovery; new member accounts are created with NO root credentials by default once enabled. A complementary 'privileged root actions' feature lets the central admin perform the handful of tasks that still require root (via sts:AssumeRoot) without standing root creds in each account. Two independently selectable capabilities: 'Root credentials management' and 'Privileged root actions in member accounts'. It is OPT-IN: requires enabling IAM trusted access in Organizations and explicit EnableOrganizationsRootCredentialsManagement / EnableOrganizationsRootSessions calls.
gap: This is a genuine strength that materially shrinks the per-account root attack surface, but it is opt-in and not retroactively default for existing orgs -- pre-existing member accounts keep their root credentials until an admin acts. A few root-only tasks still need per-account password recovery. The management account's own root is not centralized away by this feature.
risk: Until enabled, every member account carries a standing root credential that bypasses SCPs (root is only partially constrained) and is a high-value target for a multi-team org -- one phished member-account root can pivot. Enabling it is the correct default for protecting IP-bearing accounts; the residual is that adoption is manual.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_root-enable-root-access.html ('After you centralize root access, you can choose to delete root user credentials from member accounts...New accounts you create in AWS Organizations have no root user credentials by default') and https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2024/11/manage-root-access-aws-identity-access-management/

probe: IAM Identity Center: central SSO, external IdP federation, permission sets across accounts
finding: IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) is the recommended workforce-identity layer: an 'organization instance' deployed in the management account federates once (SAML 2.0) to an external IdP (Entra ID, Okta, Ping, Google) or uses a built-in identity store, then assigns 'permission sets' (provisioned as IAM roles) to users/groups across many member accounts from one place. Supports SCIM 2.0 auto-provisioning of users and groups, an access portal, multi-region replication, and trusted identity propagation to AWS-managed apps. The combined accounts-or-applications quota per organization instance is 3000 (raisable) — e.g. 2750 accounts + 250 apps; this differs from an 'account instance,' a separate Identity Center concept (one per AWS account) that does not support permission sets.
gap: Strong central-identity story and the correct default for a multi-team org. The friction is that account access via permission sets still resolves to per-account IAM roles, so least-privilege must be authored per permission set and the account remains the assignment unit -- the SSO layer simplifies federation but does not remove the account-sprawl management burden.
risk: Broadly positive: federating one IdP and removing per-account IAM users sharply reduces credential sprawl. Residual risk is operational -- mis-scoped permission sets propagate the same over-permission to every assigned account at once.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html ('One place to assign permissions to multiple AWS accounts'; 'one point of federation'; SAML IdP and SCIM support) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/limits.html

probe: IAM Identity Center SCIM provisioning limitations: nested groups and multivalue attributes
finding: SCIM provisioning into Identity Center has documented structural limits: nested groups are NOT supported (a group within a group is not expanded), and multivalue attributes (e.g., multiple emails/phone numbers) are not provisioned and will fail. With Entra ID specifically, the provisioning service does not recursively unpack indirectly-assigned (nested) group members -- only direct members of an explicitly assigned group are synced. This forces a flattened group structure on the AWS side.
gap: Orgs that model team membership as nested AD/Entra groups (common in large multi-team enterprises) cannot mirror that hierarchy into Identity Center; they must flatten groups, which fragments the source-of-truth and risks membership drift between IdP and AWS. This is a real but under-advertised constraint relative to the 'just sync your IdP' framing.
risk: Flattening or manual group maintenance creates windows where an offboarded engineer remains in an AWS-side group (because the nested membership change in the IdP did not propagate), prolonging access to accounts holding proprietary IP. Access-review accuracy degrades when AWS groups diverge from IdP group structure.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/developerguide/limitations.html (SCIM specification limitations) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/idp-microsoft-entra.html (Entra does not provision nested-group members recursively)

probe: AWS Control Tower as landing-zone / guardrail orchestration layer
finding: Control Tower is an orchestration layer ON TOP of Organizations (plus Service Catalog and IAM Identity Center) that stands up a best-practice landing zone in under an hour: it creates a Log Archive account and an Audit account, centralizes CloudTrail/Config, vends accounts via Account Factory, and applies 'controls' (guardrails) of three kinds -- preventive (SCPs), detective (Config rules), proactive (CloudFormation hooks) -- in three guidance tiers: mandatory, strongly recommended, elective. It includes drift detection to flag divergence from the baseline. As of Nov 2024 it also offers configurable managed controls implemented via RCPs.
gap: Control Tower is itself OPT-IN and a separate adoption step -- it is not the default state of an Organization, so orgs that build directly on Organizations get none of the mandatory guardrails, the Log Archive/Audit accounts, or drift detection unless they adopt it (or rebuild equivalents). Only the 'mandatory' control tier is enforced by default within Control Tower; the strongly-recommended and elective controls (which include many hardening rules) are opt-in. Control Tower account quota is 10,000 and not increasable.
risk: An org that scales accounts without Control Tower (or an equivalent landing-zone framework) has no enforced baseline -- each team account can drift independently, and there is no central drift signal. Even with Control Tower, relying on only mandatory controls leaves recommended hardening (e.g., many detective Config rules) off by default.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/controltower/latest/userguide/what-is-control-tower.html ('orchestrates the capabilities of several other AWS services, including AWS Organizations...to build a landing zone'; controls are preventive/detective/proactive and mandatory/strongly recommended/elective; 'to help keep your organizations and accounts from drift') and https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2024/11/aws-control-tower-configurable-resource-control-policies/

probe: Delegated administration for security services -- breadth, but security telemetry is opt-in not default-on
finding: Organizations supports delegating administration of security services to a dedicated security/audit account (not the management account) for GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config, IAM Access Analyzer, Macie, Detective, Inspector, Audit Manager, Firewall Manager, CloudTrail, and centralized root access -- the right pattern for separating security operations from the billing/management account. GuardDuty and Security Hub support an 'auto-enable for new accounts' preference so accounts vended later are automatically onboarded. HOWEVER, none of these services is enabled by default at the org level: the delegated admin must explicitly turn on the service AND set the auto-enable (ALL or NEW) preference; new accounts are not protected until that configuration exists.
gap: The delegated-admin model is well-architected and broadly supported, but the default state of a brand-new AWS account/organization is NO threat detection, NO posture management, NO data-classification scanning -- GuardDuty, Security Hub, Macie, Inspector are all off until someone enables them and sets auto-enable. The footgun is misconfiguration-by-omission: forget to set 'auto-enable: ALL/NEW' and freshly vended accounts silently run blind.
risk: For a multi-team org spinning up accounts for GPU training jobs and code repos, any account created before (or outside) the auto-enable configuration has no GuardDuty (no credential-exfil/crypto-mining detection) and no Macie (no detection of sensitive IP/PII landing in S3). Detection coverage becomes a function of operator diligence per account rather than an org-wide default.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/set-guardduty-auto-enable-preferences.html (auto-enable preference ALL/NEW/NONE for new accounts) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/security-reference-architecture/security-tooling.html (delegated-admin support across GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config, Macie, Inspector, etc.)

probe: Data-perimeter triad (SCP + RCP + VPC endpoint policy) as a coordinated, closed perimeter — prescribed pattern, condition-key coverage, and ergonomics of composing 'credentials can't be used from outside our network' + 'data can't be copied to an attacker's account'
finding: AWS explicitly prescribes the data perimeter as a coordinated triad, not three isolated controls. The IAM 'Establish permissions guardrails using data perimeters' page and the 'Building a Data Perimeter on AWS' whitepaper define a 3x2 matrix of six control objectives across three perimeters (identity / resource / network), mapping each cell to a specific policy type and condition key: (1) 'only trusted identities can access my resources' -> RCP on resources with aws:PrincipalOrgID; (2) 'only trusted identities from my network' -> VPC endpoint policy with aws:PrincipalOrgID; (3) 'my identities access only trusted resources' -> SCP on identities with aws:ResourceOrgID; (4) 'only trusted resources from my network' -> VPC endpoint policy with aws:ResourceOrgID; (5) 'my identities access resources only from expected networks' -> SCP with aws:SourceVpc/aws:SourceIp; (6) 'my resources accessed only from expected networks' -> RCP with aws:SourceVpc/aws:VpceOrgID. The whitepaper is explicit that the three controls compose by design (e.g., RCP+VPCe enforcing aws:PrincipalOrgID 'indirectly accomplishes the same outcome' as putting aws:ResourceOrgID on the endpoint, calling the overlap 'defense in depth'). AWS publishes a ready-to-use policy repo (aws-samples/data-perimeter-policy-examples) and the 'data perimeter helper' tool to size the blast radius from CloudTrail before enforcing. The pattern is real, well-specified, and the SCP+VPCe legs cover the entire IAM-authorized service surface. What makes it 'partial' rather than 'present' for this org: the RCP leg — the half that enforces 'untrusted external identities cannot touch OUR resources' and 'our resources only reachable from our networks' — only applies to a finite, enumerated subset of services (see companion probe), so the closed perimeter is only fully closed for the SCP-covered identity-and-network legs, while the resource-side legs have service gaps.
gap: The triad is entirely customer-built and opt-in: every control (SCP type, RCP type, and per-endpoint VPC endpoint policies) must be explicitly enabled, authored, tested, and attached — none ships enforcing. A new AWS Organization has zero perimeter; the default RCPFullAWSAccess / FullAWSAccess policies allow everything. There is no AWS-managed 'turn on the org perimeter' switch; the org must compose ~all six matrix cells correctly across SCPs, RCPs, and a VPC endpoint policy on every interface/gateway endpoint in every account and Region, plus maintain an exception list for AWS service principals (CloudTrail, CloudFormation FAS, etc.) that legitimately cross the boundary. Authoring is error-prone enough that AWS ships a dedicated helper tool and warns against attaching RCPs at the org root without staged testing. For a multi-team org, the perimeter is only as closed as its weakest-authored account/endpoint.
risk: For a multi-team org whose value is proprietary IP in S3/ECR plus model weights and source code, the perimeter is the control that actually guarantees 'our credentials can't be used from outside our network' and 'our data can't be copied to an attacker's account.' Because it is opt-in and per-endpoint, the realistic failure mode is a partially-deployed perimeter: SCPs blocking exfil to external resources on identities, but one team's VPC endpoint missing its policy, or RCPs never enabled — leaving a hole where compromised credentials used off-network, or a resource-based policy granting an outside account, is not blocked. Evaluating SCP, RCP, and PrivateLink as separate inventory items (as the draft did) hides that they only deliver a closed perimeter when all three are composed and uniformly deployed.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_data-perimeters.html (six control objectives matrix mapping policy type + condition key per perimeter dimension); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/building-a-data-perimeter-on-aws/perimeter-implementation.html ('You'll use RCPs and VPC endpoint policies to constrain which principals are allowed access... You'll use SCPs and VPC endpoint policies to constrain which resources... You'll use SCPs and RCPs to constrain which networks'; 'defense in depth' composition statements)

probe: RCP service coverage — does the resource-side leg of the data perimeter (aws:PrincipalOrgID / aws:SourceVpc enforced ON your resources) actually cover the services holding this org's proprietary IP: Bedrock, SageMaker, EC2/EBS, Lambda, RDS, EKS?
finding: RCPs — the only policy type that enforces 'no untrusted external identity can access MY resource' and 'my resource is only reachable from expected networks' directly on the resource — support only an enumerated subset of services. As of the AWS Organizations RCP doc, the list is ~24 services: S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager, Cognito, CloudWatch Logs, DynamoDB, AppConfig, AppStream, EC2 Auto Scaling, CodeBuild, CodeCommit, Comprehend, Comprehend Medical, DAX, Amazon ECR, AWS Health, Kinesis Video Streams, OpenSearch Serverless, Support, Textract, Transcribe, Translate. Crucially for an AI/GPU org: Amazon Bedrock is NOT in the list; Amazon SageMaker is NOT in the list; EC2 instances/EBS, Lambda, RDS, and EKS are NOT in the list (only EC2 Auto Scaling appears). So the IP that sits in S3 and ECR IS covered by an RCP resource-side perimeter; but model weights/endpoints accessed via Bedrock and SageMaker, and compute/data in EC2/EBS/RDS/Lambda/EKS, are NOT protected by the RCP leg. AWS's documented fallback is twofold: (a) for resources without RCP support, attach resource-based policies directly (only works for services that HAVE resource-based policies — Bedrock and SageMaker have limited/uneven resource-policy support, EC2/EBS have none); and (b) rely on the SCP + VPC-endpoint-policy legs, which DO cover the full IAM-authorized surface, to indirectly bound these services. RCPs also have hard carve-outs: they never apply to the management account, never apply to service-linked roles, and never apply to AWS managed KMS keys.
gap: The resource-side perimeter is non-uniform across exactly the services that matter most to an AI/IP org. Bedrock (custom/fine-tuned model access, model invocation), SageMaker (training jobs, notebook/Studio access, model registry), and the core compute/database tier (EC2/EBS snapshots, RDS, Lambda, EKS) cannot be wrapped in an RCP that says 'only my org's principals, only from my network.' The documented mitigation — SCP + VPC endpoint policy — protects the identity-and-network legs but does NOT replicate the resource-side guarantee that a resource-based policy (e.g., a Bedrock or SageMaker resource granting an external account) cannot open a hole; SCPs constrain YOUR identities, not what an outside principal can do against your resource via a too-permissive resource policy. The service-linked-role and management-account carve-outs further mean SLR-mediated paths and anything in the management account sit entirely outside the RCP perimeter.
risk: An org can correctly enable RCPs org-wide and believe it has closed 'data can't be copied to an attacker's account,' yet a Bedrock custom-model share, a SageMaker model-registry or notebook resource policy, an EBS/RDS snapshot shared cross-account, or an EKS-fronted workload remains outside RCP enforcement. For proprietary model weights (SageMaker/Bedrock) and source code (S3/ECR — these two ARE covered), the perimeter is strongest exactly where the artifacts are version-controlled and weakest exactly where the trained models live and run. The footgun is that 'RCPs are enabled' reads as a closed perimeter, while the highest-value AI assets fall through the service-coverage gap and must be backstopped by SCP deny-on-sharing-actions plus detective controls (Config/EventBridge remediation) that AWS documents precisely because the preventive RCP leg is absent for them.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_rcps.html ('List of AWS services that support RCPs' — enumerated list excludes Bedrock, SageMaker, EC2 instances, Lambda, RDS, EKS; 'RCPs do not impact the effective permissions of any service-linked role'; 'RCPs do not affect resources in the management account'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_data-perimeters.html ('To enforce data perimeter controls on resources that are currently not supported by RCPs, you can use resource-based policies that are attached to resources directly'); https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2026/02/aws-expands-resource-control-policies-amazon/ (DynamoDB added Feb 2026, confirming list is still incrementally expanding)

probe: EBS encryption-by-default: account/region setting, default state, and CMK binding for proprietary volumes/snapshots holding model weights and training data
finding: Amazon EBS encryption-by-default is OFF by default and is a per-Region, per-account setting (`ec2:EnableEbsEncryptionByDefault` / console EC2 > Settings > Data protection and security). AWS docs state plainly: 'Encryption by default is a Region-specific setting. If you enable it for a Region, you cannot disable it for individual volumes or snapshots in that Region.' Until explicitly enabled in each Region, newly created EBS volumes and instance root/data volumes are created UNENCRYPTED. When enabled you can bind it to a chosen symmetric customer-managed KMS key, otherwise it falls back to the AWS managed key `aws/ebs`. Two critical caveats: (1) 'Encryption by default has no effect on existing EBS volumes or snapshots' — flipping it on does not retroactively encrypt anything; (2) it does NOT block someone from explicitly creating an unencrypted volume in a Region where the setting is off, and there is no org-wide single switch — it must be set in every Region of every account independently.
