This is the genuine GPU/IP gap: SageMaker, EKS, and EC2 GPU instances persist model weights, checkpoints, and training data on EBS volumes and snapshots, and those snapshots are the share/exfil surface (an unencrypted snapshot can even be made PUBLIC: 'You can share only unencrypted snapshots publicly. The encrypted snapshot cannot be made public.').
gap: There is no Organization-level, default-on enforcement of EBS encryption. The control is opt-in, per-Region, and per-account, so a new account or a newly enabled Region (e.g., a team spinning up GPU capacity in a region nobody hardened) silently produces unencrypted volumes and snapshots. AWS provides no native 'encrypt all EBS everywhere' org policy — closing the gap requires either an SCP denying ec2:CreateVolume/RunInstances/CopySnapshot when ec2:Encrypted=false (Discoverable, must be hand-written), a Config rule + remediation, or a per-Region/per-account bootstrap that calls enable-ebs-encryption-by-default in all ~30 regions. The retroactive blind spot (existing volumes/snapshots stay unencrypted) means turning it on does not remediate the back catalog of checkpoints already on disk.
risk: A multi-team org with proprietary model weights faces a direct IP-leak path: a team in an un-hardened region launches a GPU instance, the root/data volume is unencrypted, a snapshot is taken, and that unencrypted snapshot can be shared cross-account or made public by mistake — leaking weights and training data in the clear. Because the setting is per-region/per-account and off by default, coverage gaps are the rule, not the exception, until an org explicitly builds enforcement.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/encryption-by-default.html (quote: 'Encryption by default is a Region-specific setting... If you enable it for a Region, you cannot disable it for individual volumes or snapshots' and 'Encryption by default has no effect on existing EBS volumes or snapshots'); public-share constraint: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-modifying-snapshot-permissions.html

probe: Tag governance as the substrate for multi-team ABAC/isolation: do Tag Policies actually force team/cost-center/data-classification tags to EXIST at resource creation, or must SCPs do the real enforcing?
finding: AWS Organizations Tag Policies do NOT prevent untagged resource creation. The AWS docs are explicit: 'Basic compliance rules do not enforce tag compliance on resources that are created without tags. This capability does not enforce missing tag keys. You cannot use this capability to ensure that required or mandatory tag keys are configured at resource creation.' Tag Policy enforcement mode only validates the VALUE and CAPITALIZATION of a tag that is actually supplied (e.g., block CostCenter unless value is in {Legal,HR}); it is silent when a tag key is absent, and 'Account administrators can still create untagged resources.' To actually require tags to exist at creation, AWS directs you to SCPs: 'Use SCPs to prevent IAM users and roles in target accounts from creating certain resource types if the request doesn't include the specified tags' — i.e., hand-written deny policies using `"Null": {"aws:RequestTag/team": "true"}` plus `aws:TagKeys`, per service and per action. ABAC itself is fully supported (aws:PrincipalTag / aws:ResourceTag condition keys), but the IAM/ABAC layer presumes tags are present and consistent — which Tag Policies alone do not guarantee. Enforcement is also limited to 'supported resource types' only.
gap: The substrate that makes per-team isolation, ABAC, and cost attribution enforceable — guaranteed-present, consistent team/cost-center/data-classification tags — is NOT delivered by the feature whose name implies it ('Tag Policies'). Real creation-time enforcement requires a separate, per-service, per-action SCP body (every Create* call across EC2, EBS, S3, SageMaker, Bedrock, EKS, RDS, Lambda must be individually denied-unless-tagged), and SCP tag conditions only bite on services/actions that support the aws:RequestTag/aws:TagKeys keys — coverage is uneven and must be audited service by service. Tag Policies cover only 'supported resource types', and untagged resources are invisible in compliance reports (they don't even show as noncompliant). This is a classic 'Documented but Misleading' familiar-term footgun: an org that adopts Tag Policies expecting mandatory tagging will discover teams can still launch untagged, cross-team-unattributable GPU resources.
risk: In a multi-team org, ABAC-based isolation (e.g., aws:ResourceTag/team must match aws:PrincipalTag/team to access model-weight buckets/volumes) silently fails open for any resource a team created without the team tag, because nothing forced the tag to exist. Untagged or mis-cased tags break per-team access boundaries, defeat data-classification-based controls, and make IP-bearing resources unattributable for cost and incident response. Closing this requires bespoke, comprehensively-maintained SCPs — and any service/action gap in those SCPs is a hole in tenant isolation.
evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-enforcement.html (quote: 'Basic compliance rules do not enforce tag compliance on resources that are created without tags... You cannot use this capability to ensure that required or mandatory tag keys are configured at resource creation. ... Use SCPs to prevent IAM users and roles in target accounts from creating certain resource types if the request doesn't include the specified tags'); best-practices confirmation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-best-practices.html (quote: 'Resources that have never had tags attached to them don't show as noncompliant in reports. Account administrators can still create untagged resources.'); SCP examples: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps_examples_tagging.html
